title: Lean and Just-in-Time
simple_html:
Lean is a way of running operations whose whole purpose is to deliver value to the customer with the least possible waste. Just-in-Time (JIT) is its most famous practice: make and move things only when they are actually needed, in the exact amount needed, not earlier and not in big piles. The ideas come from the Toyota Production System in post-war Japan.

The core idea: hunt waste

Lean defines waste (muda) as anything that uses resources but does not add value the customer would pay for. The classic list is the seven wastes: overproduction, waiting, transport, over-processing, inventory, motion, and defects (many add an eighth: unused talent). The lean mindset is to keep removing these, forever.

Why "just in time" works

Holding lots of inventory feels safe, but lean sees it as a problem: it ties up cash and, crucially, it hides problems. The famous metaphor is a boat on water — inventory is the high water that hides rocks (machine breakdowns, defects, slow changeovers). Lower the water (cut inventory) and the rocks appear, forcing you to fix them.

How it actually runs

  • Pull, not push: a step only makes something when the next step asks for it, usually signalled by a card or container called a kanban.
  • Small batches and quick changeovers so you can make a little of many things, often.
  • Built-in quality so defects are caught immediately, not passed downstream.
  • Steady, level workload rather than feast-and-famine.

Key takeaways

  • Lean = maximise customer value, minimise waste; JIT = produce only what's needed, when needed.
  • The seven wastes are the checklist for improvement.
  • Low inventory exposes problems so they get solved — that's a feature, not a bug.
  • Kanban and pull systems coordinate flow without big central schedules.
  • Lean is a continuous-improvement culture, not a one-time project.
intermediate_html:
Lean is an operating philosophy aimed at maximising customer value while systematically eliminating waste; Just-in-Time (JIT) is the flagship set of techniques that delivers material and information exactly when needed, in exactly the quantity needed. Originating in the Toyota Production System (TPS), lean has become the dominant improvement paradigm across manufacturing and, increasingly, services and software.

The two pillars of TPS

Toyota describes its system as resting on two pillars: Just-in-Time (flow, pull, takt) and Jidoka (automation with a human touch — machines and people that stop instantly when a defect occurs so problems are fixed at source). Both stand on a foundation of stability and standardised work and a culture of continuous improvement (kaizen).

The seven wastes (muda)

The diagnostic heart of lean is Ohno's seven wastes: Overproduction (making too much/too early — considered the worst), Waiting, Transport, over-Processing, Inventory, Motion, and Defects (mnemonic varies; some teach TIMWOOD). An eighth — unused human talent — is now standard. Lean activity is largely the relentless identification and removal of these.

Pull, kanban, and the inventory-as-water metaphor

JIT replaces push scheduling (make to a central plan and push downstream) with pull: downstream consumption authorises upstream production via kanban signals (cards, containers, or electronic signals). The classic metaphor: inventory is water in a river, and problems (breakdowns, defects, long setups) are rocks on the bed. High inventory hides the rocks; lowering inventory deliberately exposes them so they must be solved. Thus low inventory is not just a cost saving but an improvement engine.

Enabling techniques

  • SMED (Single-Minute Exchange of Die) — Shingo's method to cut changeover times so small batches become economical, attacking the EOQ rationale for big batches at its root.
  • 5S — Sort, Set in order, Shine, Standardise, Sustain: workplace organisation that makes abnormalities visible.
  • Heijunka — production levelling to smooth volume and mix, reducing internal bullwhip.
  • Takt time — the rhythm of customer demand (available time / demand) that paces the line.
  • Poka-yoke — mistake-proofing so errors cannot occur or are caught instantly (part of jidoka).

Worked example: takt time and kanban sizing

A cell works 8 hours (480 minutes), with 30 minutes of breaks, so available time is 450 minutes. Daily customer demand is 90 units. Takt time = 450/90 = 5 minutes: the cell must complete one unit every 5 minutes to match demand — no faster (overproduction) and no slower (shortfall). For kanban sizing, the number of cards roughly equals (demand during lead time + safety) / container size. If demand is 90/day, replenishment lead time is 0.5 day, safety factor 10%, and each container holds 10 units: cards = (90 x 0.5 x 1.1)/10 = 4.95, round up to 5 kanban cards. Fewer cards = less inventory but less buffer against variability — the lever that exposes rocks.

Lean vs MRP (push)

Lean / JIT (pull)MRP (push)
TriggerActual downstream demandForecast + master schedule
InventoryMinimised, visibleBuffered, planned
Best forStable, repetitive demandVariable, complex BOMs, long lead times

In practice many firms run hybrids, using MRP for planning and material acquisition while running pull/kanban on the shop floor.

How to apply it

Start by mapping the value stream and tagging each step as value-adding or one of the seven wastes; compute takt time to expose imbalance; pilot a pull loop with kanban on a stable product; cut setup times via SMED so small batches pay off; and institutionalise kaizen so gains are sustained. Remember lean is a culture and a journey, not a toolkit applied once.
full_html:
Lean is a comprehensive operating philosophy whose objective is to maximise the value delivered to the customer while minimising waste, variability, and inflexibility. Just-in-Time (JIT) is its central logistical principle: produce and convey only what is needed, when it is needed, in the amount needed. Together they constitute the most influential operations paradigm of the past half-century, exported far beyond their origin in Japanese automotive manufacturing into healthcare, services, software (Agile/Kanban), and start-up methodology. This lesson develops lean's intellectual lineage, its core principles and tools, the mathematics of JIT flow, the debates surrounding it, and its links to quality (Lesson 8), supply chains (Lesson 6), and planning and control (Lesson 5).

Origins and intellectual lineage

Lean grew out of the Toyota Production System (TPS), developed principally by Taiichi Ohno and Eiji Toyoda after 1945, with Shigeo Shingo contributing key engineering methods (Ohno, 1988; Shingo, 1985). Toyota's constraints — scarce capital, small fragmented markets, no room for inventory — made the American mass-production model of long runs and large buffers untenable, forcing an alternative built on flow and waste elimination. The Western term "lean" was coined by John Krafcik (1988) and popularised by the MIT International Motor Vehicle Program study The Machine That Changed the World (Womack, Jones & Roos, 1990), which documented Japanese plants achieving roughly half the labour hours, half the defects, and far less inventory than Western counterparts. Womack and Jones (1996) later distilled lean into five principles: specify value from the customer's perspective; map the value stream; create flow; let the customer pull; and pursue perfection.

The architecture of TPS: two pillars

TPS is conventionally drawn as a "house." The two pillars are Just-in-Time (continuous flow, pull via kanban, and pacing to takt time) and Jidoka ("automation with a human touch": equipment and operators empowered to stop the line the instant an abnormality appears, so defects are never passed downstream and root causes are fixed). The foundation is stability and standardised work, plus heijunka (levelling); the roof is the goal of best quality, lowest cost, shortest lead time. The animating culture is kaizen — continuous, incremental, employee-driven improvement — and respect for people, which Liker (2004) argues is the half of TPS Western adopters most often omit.

Waste: muda, mura, muri

Lean targets three enemies. Muda (waste) is the famous list of seven non-value-adding activities (Ohno, 1988): overproduction, waiting, transportation, over-processing, inventory, motion, and defects — with unused employee creativity now widely added as an eighth. Overproduction is regarded as the gravest waste because it generates the others (it creates inventory, which needs transport and storage and hides defects). Mura is unevenness/variability in demand or flow, and muri is overburden of people or equipment; heijunka attacks mura, and balanced standardised work attacks muri. A mature lean view sees muda as often a symptom of underlying mura and muri.

The inventory-as-water metaphor and why low stock is desirable

The defining philosophical move of JIT is to treat inventory not as an asset that buffers risk but as a waste that conceals problems. The canonical metaphor: a boat sails on a river whose water level is inventory; beneath the surface lie rocks — machine breakdowns, long setups, quality defects, unreliable suppliers, absenteeism. High water lets the boat sail over the rocks (problems stay hidden); deliberately lowering the water exposes a rock, which must then be removed. JIT therefore uses inventory reduction as a diagnostic and forcing mechanism for continuous improvement, the opposite of the traditional safety-stock instinct (and a deliberate counterpoint to the EOQ logic of Lesson 5).

Pull systems and kanban

Whereas push systems (MRP) schedule production against a forecast and push output downstream, pull systems authorise production only in response to actual downstream consumption. The signalling device is the kanban (Japanese for "signboard"): a card, container, or electronic signal that, when a downstream station empties a bin, travels upstream to authorise replenishment of exactly that quantity. The number of kanban in circulation caps work-in-process — fewer cards mean less inventory and more exposed rocks. This ties directly to Little's Law (WIP = throughput x flow time): for a given throughput, cutting WIP cuts flow time, so lean's relentless WIP reduction is mathematically a lead-time reduction strategy.

Core techniques

  • SMED (Single-Minute Exchange of Die), Shingo (1985): separate internal setup (machine stopped) from external (machine running) and convert the former to the latter, slashing changeover times from hours to minutes. This destroys the economic case for large batches and makes one-piece flow feasible.
  • 5S: Sort, Set in order, Shine, Standardise, Sustain — workplace organisation that surfaces abnormalities and underpins standardised work.
  • Heijunka: levelling volume and mix to smooth the demand signal internally, reducing the self-inflicted bullwhip of Lesson 6.
  • Takt time: net available time / customer demand — the heartbeat that synchronises production to the customer.
  • Poka-yoke: mistake-proofing devices that prevent or instantly detect errors, operationalising jidoka.
  • Andon and the "stop the line" authority: visual management plus empowerment to halt for quality.

Worked calculation

Consider a final-assembly cell with 480 minutes/shift, two 10-minute breaks (460 net), and demand of 92 units/shift. Takt time = 460/92 = 5.0 min/unit. If the total manual work content is 20 minutes, the theoretical minimum number of workstations to balance the line is work content / takt = 20/5 = 4 stations (line balancing, Lesson 4). For the supermarket feeding this cell, with daily demand D = 92, replenishment lead time L = 0.25 shift, a safety factor of 20%, and container size c = 8: number of kanban = (D x L x (1 + safety)) / c = (92 x 0.25 x 1.2)/8 = 3.45 -> 4 cards, capping WIP at 32 units. Halving the lead time (via SMED and proximity) would roughly halve the cards and the WIP — concretely lowering the water level.

Common mistakes and misconceptions

  • "Lean = cutting headcount." Lean removes waste, not people; treating it as a layoff programme destroys the kaizen culture it depends on (Liker, 2004).
  • Copying tools without the system. Bolting on kanban or 5S without jidoka, standardised work, and respect-for-people yields fragile, short-lived gains.
  • Applying JIT to unstable demand or supply. Pure JIT assumes reasonable stability; with high mura it needs heijunka or buffers first — naive zero-inventory invites stockouts.
  • Ignoring variability. Lean and Six Sigma are complementary (Lesson 8): reducing variation is a precondition for low buffers.
  • Confusing lean with always-cheapest. Lean optimises value and flow, which sometimes justifies investment (e.g., extra changeover capability).

Debates and the resilience critique

The most pointed contemporary debate concerns lean vs resilience. Ultra-lean, single-sourced, low-inventory global supply chains proved fragile during disruptions (the 2011 Tohoku earthquake, the COVID-19 pandemic, semiconductor shortages), prompting calls for strategic buffers and dual sourcing. Defenders counter that the failures stemmed from misapplied lean — eliminating strategic buffers, not waste — and that true lean, which prizes stability and supplier development, is compatible with resilience. A second debate concerns lean in services and knowledge work, where "waste" and "flow" are harder to define, though Agile and Kanban software methods are direct descendants. Empirically, Shah and Ward (2003, 2007) helped formalise lean's bundles of practices and showed performance depends on adopting them as a coherent system rather than piecemeal.

Cross-links

Lean attacks the order-batching and lead-time drivers of the bullwhip effect (Lesson 6), depends on and reinforces quality and continuous improvement (Lesson 8), contrasts with MRP push planning (Lesson 5), shapes process design and line balancing (Lesson 4), and its resilience trade-offs connect to managing risk and failure (Lesson 9). Strategically, lean must be chosen to fit the firm's competitive priorities (Lesson 3).
